description: Topology-as-Logic validation on the ISCAS85 c432 benchmark circuit (27-channel interrupt controller). 36 primary inputs, 7 primary outputs, 160 gates. Layer 1 (physical_wiring): structural gate connections from the published .bench netlist. Layer 2 (state_correlation): Pearson correlation of gate activation over 2000 Monte Carlo random input patterns (seed=42). Primary hypothesis: betweenness centrality persistence across layers — identifying load-bearing logic nodes regardless of fan-in degree. Secondary hypothesis: degree hub persistence, predicted to be weaker than betweenness based on findings from H_LOGIC_DIGITAL_v1.

hypotheses.statement: Betweenness centrality of nodes in the physical_wiring layer is positively correlated with betweenness centrality in the state_correlation layer (Pearson r > 0.40). Nodes that lie on critical paths in the structural wiring graph also mediate information flow in the simulation state-correlation graph. This tests whether the load-bearing logic signal found in H_LOGIC_DIGITAL_v1 (betweenness r=0.771) replicates at larger circuit scale.

The cross-layer hub correlation (degree-based) between physical_wiring and state_correlation is positive but weaker than the betweenness-based persistence measured by h1. In H_LOGIC_DIGITAL_v1, degree hub-persistence collapsed to near-zero while betweenness persisted strongly. For a more complex circuit, degree may recover some signal but should remain below the betweenness correlation.

The number of nodes showing rank divergence greater than 20 positions between physical_wiring degree rank and state_correlation degree rank is at least 30 (out of 196 nodes). Large rank gaps identify nodes that are structurally prominent but operationally peripheral (or vice versa) — the topology-as-logic divergence signal.
